title: The factorial structure of the general aptitude test battery
abstract:
THE FACTORIAL STRUCTURE OF THE 
APTITUDE TEST BATTERY 
FRASER N. WATTS 
Kin$# Cohge Hoapital, Landon’ 
AND 
BRIAN 8. EVERITT 
GENERAL 
In&ituls of Psychida(, UnivM.sily of London 
Reexamined the correlational structure of the General Aptitude Teat Battery 
to determine whether it permits the measurement of nine separate aptitudes, 
as is claimed. The attern of correlations between subteata suggeata that 
several of the aptitu&a are of doubtful validity. Three matrices of correlations 
between tests were factor analyzed using the maximum likelihood method. 
’ A teat of determinancy indicated that in no case was it poseible to extract 
more than three adequately determined factors. 
The General Aptitude Test Battery (GATB) is one of the most wisely used 
of a group of psychological tests that purport to measure a S’s performance on a 
series of different aptitudes. It contains 12 subtests, which are combined to yield 
scores on nine aptitudes (cf. Table 1). This paper will reexamine the correlational 
and factor-analytic structure of the subtests and argue that it does not yield the 
nine aptitudes that the GATB purports to measure. It will be pointed out, for 
example, that in the published correlation matrix of the current B-1002 version 
of the test there are cases of pairs of tests, purporting to measure the same aptitude,

Journal of Clinical Psychology, July, 1980, Vol. 36, No. 8. 
that have lower correlations with each other than they have with other subtests 
in the battery. This situation seems largely attributable to an under-critical use 
of factor analysis in the construction of the GATB. 
DEVELOPMENT 
OF THE GATB 
The original factor analytic work on which the GATB is based has been de- 
scribed in an extensive pa er published by the Staff of the Division of Occupational 
Analysis (1945). Eight bfferent factor analyses were carried out, using a total 
of 59 tests. However, t k s e original factor analyses have a number of unsatisfactory 
features. 
ber of tests in these original studies ranged from 15-27, there 
was no study that contained all the tests eventually selected for the GATB. There 
was, therefore, no opportunity to verify that the selected group of subtests had 
the assumed factor-analytic structure. Also, in many cases, the sample size was 
too small to allow complete confidence in the replicability of the factor analyses. 
There were only two studies with a sample size of over 500. The sample size in 
the other studies ranged from 98 to 275. 
The test constructors seem to have been aware of this problem. In the intro- 
duction to the unpublished report of the Group 7 study (Jasper, 1944), it is suggested 
that this particular study should be considered the most important because it 
has the largest sample and the most diversified sample of tests. Even so, the study 
contains only 9 of the 12 subtests that constitute the current B-1002 version of 
the GATB. This Group 7 study will be selected for a closer examination of the 
factor-analytic methods employed in the construction of the GATB. 
As in all the other studies, the centroid method of factor analysis was employed. 
Eight factors were extracted (general intellectual, finger dexterity, aiming, nu- 
merical, spatial, verbal, manual dexterity, and perceptual). Clerical perception 
and motor speed factors were apparently not found (Jasper, 1944). It, seems doubt- 
ful whether all the factors were identified correctly by the designations given 
them, or indeed whether it was possible to extract as many as eight adequately 
determined factors. 
The most unsatisfactory one was the so-called finger dexterity factor, on 
which the loadings of the manual dexterity tests were also substantial (.42 in 
each case) and not much lower than the loadings of the finger dexterity tests (.63 
and 33). The second finger dexterity test also had a substantial (.40) loading on 
the manual dexterity factor. This problem is commented on by Jasper (1944). 
There are other cases in which an inspection of the correlation matrix suggests 
that the distinctions between pairs of factors are not very secure. For example, 
arithmetic reasoning correlates with computation (the other numerical test in the 
GATB) only slightly higher (.70) than it does with the main verbal test (same 
and opposites) included in this study (.62). Similarly, tool-matching, a perceptual 
test, correlates almost as highly with the other spatial tests as it does with the 
other perceptual tests. The factor analysis adopted thus provides a less than con- 
vincing solution of the correlation matrix. 
One of the basic problems seems to be the attempt to extract too many factors. 
Several of the smaller factors were probably relatively poorly determined and 
accounted for very little variance. If a number of such factors are included in a 
rotation, then even the first few factors become relatively insubstantial. Such 
small factors are unlikely to be exactly replicable. It is important, if scientific 
validity is to be claimed for a particular set of factors, that they should achieve 
invariance, i.e., that the tests should have a similar pattern of factor loadings 
despite moderate changes in the test battery and moderate changes in the tested 
population (cf. Gorsuch, 1974, Chapter 16). Such invariance is more likely to be 
achieved if a limited number of adequately determined factors are extracted. 
The maximum likelihood method of factor analysis, originally suggested 
by Lawley (1940) and later developed into a practical alternative to other forms

Reexamined the correlational structure of the General Aptitude Teat Battery
to determine whether it permits the measurement of nine separate aptitudes,
as is claimed. The attern of correlations between subteata suggeata that
several of the aptitu&a are of doubtful validity. Three matrices of correlations
between tests were factor analyzed using the maximum likelihood method.
’ A teat of determinancy indicated that in no case was it poseible to extract
more than three adequately determined factors.
The General Aptitude Test Battery (GATB) is one of the most wisely used
of a group of psychological tests that purport to measure a S’s performance on a
series of different aptitudes. It contains 12 subtests, which are combined to yield
scores on nine aptitudes (cf. Table 1). This paper will reexamine the correlational
and factor-analytic structure of the subtests and argue that it does not yield the
nine aptitudes that the GATB purports to measure. It will be pointed out, for
example, that in the published correlation matrix of the current B-1002 version
of the test there are cases of pairs of tests, purporting to measure the same aptitude,

that have lower correlations with each other than they have with other subtests
in the battery. This situation seems largely attributable to an under-critical use
of factor analysis in the construction of the GATB.
The original factor analytic work on which the GATB is based has been de-
scribed in an extensive pa er published by the Staff of the Division of Occupational
Analysis (1945). Eight bfferent factor analyses were carried out, using a total
of 59 tests. However, t k s e original factor analyses have a number of unsatisfactory
ber of tests in these original studies ranged from 15-27, there
was no study that contained all the tests eventually selected for the GATB. There
was, therefore, no opportunity to verify that the selected group of subtests had
the assumed factor-analytic structure. Also, in many cases, the sample size was
too small to allow complete confidence in the replicability of the factor analyses.
There were only two studies with a sample size of over 500. The sample size in
the other studies ranged from 98 to 275.
The test constructors seem to have been aware of this problem. In the intro-
duction to the unpublished report of the Group 7 study (Jasper, 1944), it is suggested
that this particular study should be considered the most important because it
has the largest sample and the most diversified sample of tests. Even so, the study
contains only 9 of the 12 subtests that constitute the current B-1002 version of
the GATB. This Group 7 study will be selected for a closer examination of the
factor-analytic methods employed in the construction of the GATB.
As in all the other studies, the centroid method of factor analysis was employed.
Eight factors were extracted (general intellectual, finger dexterity, aiming, nu-
merical, spatial, verbal, manual dexterity, and perceptual). Clerical perception
and motor speed factors were apparently not found (Jasper, 1944). It, seems doubt-
ful whether all the factors were identified correctly by the designations given
them, or indeed whether it was possible to extract as many as eight adequately
The most unsatisfactory one was the so-called finger dexterity factor, on
which the loadings of the manual dexterity tests were also substantial (.42 in
each case) and not much lower than the loadings of the finger dexterity tests (.63
and 33). The second finger dexterity test also had a substantial (.40) loading on
the manual dexterity factor. This problem is commented on by Jasper (1944).
There are other cases in which an inspection of the correlation matrix suggests
that the distinctions between pairs of factors are not very secure. For example,
arithmetic reasoning correlates with computation (the other numerical test in the
GATB) only slightly higher (.70) than it does with the main verbal test (same
and opposites) included in this study (.62). Similarly, tool-matching, a perceptual
test, correlates almost as highly with the other spatial tests as it does with the
other perceptual tests. The factor analysis adopted thus provides a less than con-
vincing solution of the correlation matrix.
One of the basic problems seems to be the attempt to extract too many factors.
Several of the smaller factors were probably relatively poorly determined and
accounted for very little variance. If a number of such factors are included in a
rotation, then even the first few factors become relatively insubstantial. Such
small factors are unlikely to be exactly replicable. It is important, if scientific
validity is to be claimed for a particular set of factors, that they should achieve
invariance, i.e., that the tests should have a similar pattern of factor loadings
despite moderate changes in the test battery and moderate changes in the tested
population (cf. Gorsuch, 1974, Chapter 16). Such invariance is more likely to be
achieved if a limited number of adequately determined factors are extracted.
The maximum likelihood method of factor analysis, originally suggested
by Lawley (1940) and later developed into a practical alternative to other forms
Factorial Structure of the GATB
of factor analysis (Joreskog & Lawley, 1968), permits a mathematical approach
to the problem of how many factors should be extracted from a correlation matrix.
On the assumption that the variates have a multivariate normal distribution
and that the sample size is relatively large, a test is available to establish the number
of factors that are required, i.e., necessary and sufficient, to account for the cor-
relations between them. However, as Maxwell (1972, p. 7) has pointed out: the
fact that a factor is significant in the statistical sense does not ensure that it can
be adequately determined, any more than a significant correlation, r, between
two variates ensures that the individual’s score on one variate can be determined
with narrow confidence limits from his score on the other. To obtain an indication
of the determinacy of each factor, Maxwell considers the correlation between
the true factor and its estimate. Assuming that such a correlation should not be
less than .9, he shows that, for the diagonal matrix he is considering, an adequately
determined factor corresponds to a matrix element of magnitude greater than
Maximum Likelihood Factor Analysis of the Group 7 Data
The correlation matrix of the Group 7 study ( N  = 594) was reanalyzed using
the maximum likelihood method. A test was carried out to determine the number
of factors required (i.e., necessary and sufficient) to account for the correlations.
In addition, the determinancy of each factor was calculated using the method
recommended by Maxwell (1972). Factor analyses with progressively increasing
numbers of factors were carried out until one was found whose factors were necessary
and sufficient to account for the correlation matrix.
Nine factors were found to be required to account for the correlations (of
which one was an almost pure age-factor). This is very close to the eight factors
originally extracted by the centroid method. However, when a test of determinancy
was applied, it was found that most of the factors were inadequately determined.
In none of these factor analyses did more than three factors meet Maxwell’s cri-
terion of determinancy (i.e., correspond to matrix elements greater than four).
Even relaxing the criterion to three did not produce more than three adequately
determined factors in any factor analysis.
The GATB manual [US Employment Service, 1970, Section 111) contains
correlational matrices for both the earlier B-1001 version and the current B-1002
version of the GATB, though no factor analyses of these matrices are given. How-
ever, even an inspection of these matrices casts doubt on whether they possess
the assumed factor structure. The distinctions between the manual dexterity
and finger dexterity factors and between the form perception and clerical per-
ception factors look particularly questionable.
In the correlation matrix for the B-1002 version (Table 6-3 of Section 111
of the manual) the two subtests that measure finger dexterity (i.e., assemble and
disassemble) have an intercorrelation of .28. This is actually lower than the cor-
relations each of these subtests has with the manual dexterity subtests, which
range from .29 to .43. The correlational data for the B-1001 version (Table 6-2)
are slightly better, but still far from satisfactory on this point. Examination of
the correlations between the subtests that measure clerical perception and form
perception also casts doubt on the validity of the distinction between these apti-
tudes. Table 6-3 shows that one of the form perception subtests (tool matching)
correlates more highly with the clerical perception subtest (name comparison)
than it does with form matching, the form perception subtest. This is also true
of the B-1001 version of the test (Tables 6-1 and 6-2).
Journal of Clinical Psychology, July, 1980, Vol. 36, No. 3.
Maximum Likelihood Factor Analysis of the GA T3
Factor analyses of correlation matrices for the B-1001 (Table 6-2) and B-1002
(Table 6-3) versions of the test were carried out using the same maximum likeli-
hood method that was used in reanalyBing the Group 7 study. In each case it
was found that the number of adequately determined factors was considerably
fewer than the number of aptitudes that the GATB claims to measure.
The B-1001 version claims to measure 10 aptitudes. It was found that nine
factors were necessary and sufficient to account for the correlation matrix (N =
4000). However, in no case was it possible to extract more than three adequately
determined factors, using Maxwell’s criterion of determinancy. Again, even ac-
cepting factors that correspond to elements of greater than three as adequately
determined did not affect this finding.
Finally, a maximum likelihood analysis was carried out on the correlational
data for the B-1002 version of the GATB ( N  = 100) provided in Table 6-3 of the
test manual (U.S. Employment Service, 1970). In this case three factors were
necessary and sufficient to account for the matrix. It was thus clear, without any
additional test of determinancy, that the matrix does not support the claimed
nine-factor structure. The results of this factor analysis are given in Table 2. The
factors might be designated symbolic, psychomotor and perceptual.
There are great dangers in the over-enthusiastic use of factor analysis by
test constructors. Horn (1967) has provided a salutary demonstration of the pos-
sible abuse of factor analysis in showing that quite meaningful factors can be
derived from artificially generated data that support no such structure. The con-
structors of the GATB seem to have extracted more factors than could be ade-
quately determined and to have approached the designation of their factors in
a way that exaggerated the degree of invariance of factors that had been achieved.
It must be admitted that the number of factors extracted by the test constructors
is quite close to the number shown here to be required to account for the correlation
matrix, using the test of significance provided by the maximum likelihood method.
However, it has been shown elsewhere that this criterion can lead to the extraction
of a relatively large number of factors (Harris & Harris, 1971). What is required
Factorial Structure of the GATB
in test construction is a set of factors that are adequately determined and likely
to be invariant. It is not necessary to account fully for the correlation matrix
The approach to factor analysis employed by the constructors of the GATB has
not resulted in a test that measures nine adequately determined and clearly dis-
tinguishable factors. The results of the analyses reported here suggest that the
test constructors might have had more success in constructing a three-aptitude
test rather than a nine-aptitude one. The results of the factor analysis of the B-1002
are strikingly similar to the first three factors of Weiner’s (1964) analysis of data
collected from a much larger sample. In both cases “arithmetic reasoning” is
the highest loading subtest on the first factor and “turn” the highest loading subtest
on the second. The third component was a perceptual one in each case, though
the highest loading subtest was form matching in Weiner’s analysis and tool match-
Such a factor structure would have reflected Vernon’s (1961) model of ability.
The first factor would be regarded as V:ed and the third k:m. Though it seems
to be widely believed, largely on the basis of the work of Fleishman and Hempel
(1956), that psychomotor ability fragments into many highly specific factors,
Vernon (1961, p. 162) has pointed out that a high proportion of variance in a
matrix of psychomotor tests can be accounted for by a single unrotated factor
and that such a factor often has been found in addition to k:m (p. 100). Furthermore,
the GATB might not have lost much predictive power by gdopting a three-aptitude
structure. The validity studies reported in the manual suggest, for example, that
success in particular occupations tends to be predicted by all or none of the psycho-
motor aptitudes. McNemar (1964) has made similar points about the predictive
failure of the Differential Aptitudes Test. However, it is not the purpose of this
short paper to suggest an alternative aptitude structure for the GATB, but rather
to draw attention to the poor scientific basis for the aptitude structure claimed
for the GATB. If, as is claimed here, there are inadequate grounds for combining
subtests to obtain aptitude scores in the way that the GATB indicates, it might
have been better to use scores on all 12 subtests separately in studying their ability
to predict occupational success. Replacing 12 subtest scores by nine aptitude
scores has little advantage of economy. The present analyses show that it is sta-
